summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the AVideo API router reflects arbitrary Origin header values in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header. Combined with the bypassed same-domain check, this allows cross-origin requests to access the API with credentials.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should show that the API response includes the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header reflecting the attacker-supplied Origin value, and that Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is set to true.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched the AVideo application, then sent a request to the API endpoint with a spoofed Origin header (https://evil.example.com). The response included Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com, confirming that the API reflects arbitrary origins without validation.
verification_method: The verification script sends a request to the API endpoint with a controlled Origin header and checks the response headers for the reflected Access-Control-Allow-Origin value. It verifies that the reflected value matches the attacker-supplied Origin exactly.
evidence_produced_by_run: The API response includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.example.com, reflecting the attacker-supplied Origin header.
The API response includes Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, HEAD, allowing all HTTP methods.
The API response includes Access-Control-Allow-Headers with multiple headers including Authorization.
The bypassSameDomainCheck=1 flag in the API endpoints disables the application's CSRF protection.
limitations_or_blockers: The full exploit chain (cross-origin credential theft via victim browser) requires a browser context which cannot be fully simulated with curl alone. The PoC demonstrates the server-side CORS misconfiguration.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the master branch, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
